description: Intersection: a large truck approaches laterally from the left at 8 m/s while a pedestrian stands ahead-left. Ported from legacy Test_scene/scenario_manager_test1.py; preview snapshot extrapolated 1 s backwards.

branches.condition: If truck1 continues maintaining its current cross-traffic trajectory and the T-drift preconditions remain valid at execution, with left corridor still occupied by the pedestrian and right corridor still clear.
branches.rationale: Physics evidence marks truck1 as the triggering unavoidable lateral conflict at execution time (TTC 1.2 s, on_collision_course true) and explicitly recommends code 6. Right corridor is free; left corridor is blocked by a vulnerable pedestrian, so code 6 best preserves clearance while rotating the rear toward the incoming truck, consistent with the rear-impact preference. Rollout 6 yields min separation 3.6 m and is HJ-acceptable, while left-leaning options sweep toward the pedestrian.

branches.condition: If truck1 accelerates or otherwise increases closing urgency while the pedestrian remains in the left corridor and the right corridor remains open.
branches.rationale: Acceleration by the triggering target does not change the hard rule: collision is unavoidable at execution time and the prescribed T-drift code remains 6 unless it violates a hard constraint. Left steering is forbidden by pedestrian occupancy. Among the available rollouts, code 6 is the only maneuver aligned with the precomputed T-drift assessment and the vehicle-structure rule to protect the side and take impact on the rear. It also outperforms no-intervention in lateral safety even though end HJ risk remains high due to inevitability.

branches.condition: If truck1 yields sufficiently that the lateral conflict is no longer the triggering unavoidable event, while the pedestrian stays ahead-left and the right corridor stays clear.
branches.rationale: When the primary cross-traffic threat yields, the t-drift obligation no longer applies. Braking alone is precomputed as sufficient for in-lane forward objects, and rollout 0 has the best direct stopping behavior among non-lane-change actions with end speed 2.0 m/s and min separation 3.16 m. Left lane changes remain blocked by the pedestrian, and rightward lane changes do not improve worst-case HJ risk enough to justify leaving the lane when braking is already sufficient.

branches.condition: If the pedestrian unexpectedly moves or a new obstacle activates on the right corridor, invalidating the currently clear right-side escape path.
branches.rationale: A new right-side blocker removes the only safe lateral escape corridor. Left side is already occupied by a vulnerable pedestrian, so side-steering would risk violating the no-steer-toward-VRU rule and could sweep into occupied space. With no verified safe lateral path, the least-harm fallback is full emergency braking, which is the only maneuver grounded by the braking evidence and avoids committing the vehicle side toward a protected road user.

branches.condition: Otherwise, if no guard above matches and the scene remains as precomputed.
branches.rationale: Default to the precomputed T-drift recommendation because the evidence states the collision with truck1 is unavoidable at execution time and code 6 provides the safer impact orientation with a clear right-side maneuver envelope. This avoids the left corridor occupied by the pedestrian and respects the vehicle-structure preference for front/rear impacts over side impacts.

reflex_trigger.maneuver: T-type drift avoidance maneuver, ending with the car perpendicular to the lane, facing right

matched_branch.condition: If truck1 continues maintaining its current cross-traffic trajectory and the T-drift preconditions remain valid at execution, with left corridor still occupied by the pedestrian and right corridor still clear.
matched_branch.rationale: Physics evidence marks truck1 as the triggering unavoidable lateral conflict at execution time (TTC 1.2 s, on_collision_course true) and explicitly recommends code 6. Right corridor is free; left corridor is blocked by a vulnerable pedestrian, so code 6 best preserves clearance while rotating the rear toward the incoming truck, consistent with the rear-impact preference. Rollout 6 yields min separation 3.6 m and is HJ-acceptable, while left-leaning options sweep toward the pedestrian.

consolidation.assessment: Code 6 did not achieve the intended primary objective (avoidance): a rear collision with the truck still occurred. Even though injuries were avoided due to low relative velocity, the maneuver was not effective in preventing physical impact. In this scene, the ‘triggering truck’ remained a collision risk despite the lateral drift.
consolidation.lesson: When TTC indicates an on-collision-course threat, treat lateral drift/rotation maneuvers as secondary unless you can demonstrate continuous escape space through the maneuver (not just at one rollout endpoint). Before committing, verify that the planned path keeps a nonzero lateral and longitudinal separation from the truck for the full maneuver duration; if the truck is rear/overlapping in the intended escape corridor, prioritize an action that directly increases longitudinal clearance (brake/stop or a smaller, time-critical steering change) rather than rotating into a perpendicular posture.
consolidation.better_alternative: Use an emergency braking + controlled steering to create longitudinal separation first (reduce speed to reestablish time gap), then make a minimal lane/heading adjustment toward the confirmed free side only after the truck’s relative closure rate is eliminated. (If a full stop was feasible within the available distance, prioritize stopping over a perpendicular rotation.)

execution_outcome: Rear collision with the truck occurred. Due to low relative velocity, no injuries were sustained.

trace.output: scene projected 0.90s forward to the anticipated execution state (risk 0.666)

output.summary: Primary threat truck1: HJ risk 0.71, TTC 1.2 s, on collision course: True. Braking alone IS sufficient for IN-LANE forward objects (stopping 6.25 m vs gap None m) — this metric does not cover lateral crossers. Left corridor occupied by pedestrian; right corridor free. T-DRIFT RULE ACTIVE: collision with truck1 is unavoidable at execution time; code 6 prescribed.

Ego is moving straight at 10 m/s toward an intersection. A pedestrian is ahead-left at close range and is the most immediate hazard due to proximity and high risk. A large truck is farther ahead-left and moving laterally downward in the BEV (toward ego's right side), but it is not the primary near-term conflict. The left side corridor is constrained by the pedestrian and truck; the right side appears more open for an evasive maneuver.

hazards.note: 16 m ahead, 4 m left; stationary; highest risk and closest conflict point.
23 m ahead, 14.4 m left; moving laterally across scene; potential future conflict but farther away.

output.occlusions: Left/forward-left space is partially blocked by the pedestrian and truck; the truck's size may occlude farther-left/forward-left hazards. Right corridor is comparatively open with no reported object nearby.

cases.scenario:
EGO: small car, v=(10.0,0.0)m/s, road=Intersection, weather=Sunny (good road conditions)
LARGE TRUCK truck1: 23.0m ahead, 14.4m left, v=(0.0,8.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
PEDESTRIAN pedestrian: 16.0m ahead, 4.0m left, v=(0.0,0.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
cases.outcome: Rear collision with the truck occurred. Due to low relative velocity, no injuries were sustained.
cases.lesson: In a close cross-traffic conflict, do not commit to a side-rotation maneuver unless you have a verified escape path; if the obstacle is already inside the stopping/evading envelope, prioritize the maneuver that maximizes full-body clearance and preserves braking while keeping the vehicle stable.

cases.scenario:
EGO: small car, v=(10.0,0.0)m/s, road=Intersection, weather=Sunny (good road conditions)
LARGE TRUCK truck1: 23.0m ahead, 14.4m left, v=(0.0,8.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
PEDESTRIAN pedestrian: 16.0m ahead, 4.0m left, v=(0.0,0.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
cases.outcome: Rear collision with the truck occurred. Due to low relative velocity, no injuries were sustained.
cases.lesson: If a collision is judged unavoidable, choose the impact orientation that minimizes lateral intrusion into populated corridors—and only execute after verifying there is clear clearance for the full maneuver rollout (not just minimum separation at the decision instant). In practice: confirm the path is unobstructed for the entire drift/rotation duration and that the closest occupied agent corridor is not swept during the maneuver.

output.overall_rationale: The decisive evidence is the precomputed t-drift assessment: truck1 is the triggering unavoidable lateral conflict at execution time, and code 6 is prescribed. The left corridor is occupied by a vulnerable pedestrian, so all left-steering branches are disallowed. The right corridor is free, so the safest evasive orientation is to rotate so the rear faces the incoming truck, minimizing side exposure. If truck1 yields and the unavoidable cross-traffic condition disappears, braking becomes the preferred fallback because braking alone is sufficient for the in-lane forward domain and has the best stopping behavior among non-lateral maneuvers. Historical cases warn against choosing T-drift without verified clearance; here that clearance exists only on the right, so code 6 is retained only while that corridor remains open and the triggering threat continues.

output.safety_argument: Revise the policy to make **full emergency braking (code 0)** the default whenever a pedestrian is present in the left corridor and the truck–ego conflict is laterally unavoidable, even if the “T-drift code 6” is precomputed. The “take the impact on the rear by rotating the nose away” logic primarily optimizes EV structure/ego survivability and does not provide a guarantee that the maneuver will not increase lateral exposure or shear risk toward the **vulnerable road user** (the pedestrian) during execution. Worst-case harm reduction requires minimizing any lateral commitment when the left side is occupied, so the safest action is to keep the vehicle’s lateral position as stable as possible while reducing speed (and thus collision energy), rather than relying on T-drift execution assumptions. Therefore: **remove/limit code 6 to only cases with verified, margin-based clearance from all VRUs throughout the maneuver**, otherwise choose code 0.
output.efficiency_argument: This policy overcommits to a T-drift (code 6) even though the only robust, evidence-backed capability is emergency braking and the scene is sunny/clear. Given the left corridor is occupied by a pedestrian, the biggest secondary-accident risk comes from rotating or steering into any path that could sweep the EV side past the VRU; mildness argues for staying in-lane. The fact that braking-alone is “sufficient” for the in-lane domain means the safer baseline is maximum braking (code 0) until/unless a verified lateral-clear escape corridor appears—otherwise you risk false triggering and rear-end/side-swipe chains. T-drift should be treated as a last resort only if the data can guarantee the pedestrian remains spatially protected during the maneuver; with only “right corridor clear” and no dynamic VRU clearance evidence, defaulting to code 0 is the more efficient, lower-secondary-accident choice.
output.resolution: No revision. The deterministic physics evidence explicitly sets t-drift preconditions met, collision unavoidable at execution time, and recommends code 6. Per the hard constraint, this branch cannot be revised to code 0/7. The left corridor is occupied by a vulnerable pedestrian, so no left-steering change is warranted, and the right corridor remains clear, supporting the prescribed code 6. Braking-alone sufficiency applies only to in-lane forward objects and does not override the lateral cross-traffic t-drift rule.

output.assessment: Code 6 did not achieve the intended primary objective (avoidance): a rear collision with the truck still occurred. Even though injuries were avoided due to low relative velocity, the maneuver was not effective in preventing physical impact. In this scene, the ‘triggering truck’ remained a collision risk despite the lateral drift.
output.lesson: When TTC indicates an on-collision-course threat, treat lateral drift/rotation maneuvers as secondary unless you can demonstrate continuous escape space through the maneuver (not just at one rollout endpoint). Before committing, verify that the planned path keeps a nonzero lateral and longitudinal separation from the truck for the full maneuver duration; if the truck is rear/overlapping in the intended escape corridor, prioritize an action that directly increases longitudinal clearance (brake/stop or a smaller, time-critical steering change) rather than rotating into a perpendicular posture.
output.better_alternative: Use an emergency braking + controlled steering to create longitudinal separation first (reduce speed to reestablish time gap), then make a minimal lane/heading adjustment toward the confirmed free side only after the truck’s relative closure rate is eliminated. (If a full stop was feasible within the available distance, prioritize stopping over a perpendicular rotation.)
